## 5.3.3(a) Peak Hour Flow Calculation The peak hour flow was calculated by first identifying the highest recorded daily wastewater volume from the 2022 to 2024 sample period. The highest daily volume observed was 1.18 MG on June 13ᵗʰ, 2022. When attributed to the number of active ERUs in 2022, it translates **25,650 gpd/ERU**. This daily volume per ERU is then converted to an average daily flow rate in gallons per minute (GPM) for a flow rate of . To estimate the peak hour flow from this highest average daily flow rate, a diurnal peaking factor is applied. This factor accounts for the typical variations in water use -----------------------0s }}, a peaking factor of {{ Peaking_Factor_Value, for instance, using the Harmon formula, Babbitt formula, or a typical range like 2.5 to 4.0 for smaller systems }} was used. Therefore, Peak Hour Flow (GPM) = (Highest Daily Flow Rate in GPM) ×[; ‘me_of_Town/Utility }}, this results in a calculated Peak Hour Flow of {{ ‘/8 }} GPM. ## 5.3.3(b) Assumptions and Simplifications The following assumptions were made in the derivation of the peak hour flow calculations: * It was assumed that contributions from {{ Commercial_Institutional_Sources_Assumption, for instance, commercial and institutional sources within the service area exhibit similar diurnal peaking patterns to residential flows, or their contribution to the overall peak flow is relatively small and thus adequately accounted for within the selected overall peaking factor }}. This is because {{ Rationale_Commercial, for instance, the service area is predominantly residential with limited large commercial/institutional users that would have significantly different peak discharge times }}. <>

Since the Infiltration and Inflow Study was completed in 2006, the City of Tekoa’s sewage collection system has undergone several infiltration and inflow (I/I) reduction projects in the last 12 years. The infiltration and inflow coming into the system has been greatly reduced but there is still significant I/I that enters the collection system, generally in the spring of the year. This Infiltration and Inflow Analysis will examine the level of reduction of infiltration and inflow that has been accomplished in the last 12 years as well as what recommended improvements still need to be completed to further reduce I/I. ## EPA Guidance on Infiltration/Inflow In 1985, the EPA provided a guidance document for Infiltration and Inflow analysis and project certification. In the document, the determination of non-excessive infiltration is identified as total flows of 120 gallons per capita per day (dry weather flow + I/I). It should be noted that after the guidance document was published, new laws were enacted for new construction to install low flow fixtures which has had an impact on dry weather sewage flows. For a community like Tekoa without significant development since the low flow fixture laws were enacted in 1992, the 120 gpcd is probably still appropriate. For the City of Tekoa, seasonal I/I plays a large part in the average daily flows in the sewer system. The following table identifies the average daily flows on a monthly basis at the Tekoa Wastewater Treatment Plant and the per-capita, per-day flows associated with each.

Mathematically, the total Wet Weather Flow (WWF) during a storm event can be expressed by considering RDII along with the base flow components:  
$$ \text{WWF} = Q_s + Q_{GWI} + Q_{RDII} $$  
Where:

* WWF = Total Wet Weather Flow (for instance, in gpd) during the event.

* \( Q_s \) (gpd) = Base Sanitary Flow (gpd) – the component from user discharges.

* \( Q_{GWI} \) (gpd) = Groundwater Infiltration (gpd). \( Q_{GWI} \) is the persistent infiltration component typically present even in dry weather, though it may increase during wet weather due to higher groundwater tables.

* \( Q_{RDII} \) (gpd) = Rainfall-Derived Infiltration and Inflow is the additional flow directly attributable to the rainfall event.

To perform the wet weather flow analysis for Tekoa’s collection system, daily precipitation data from the Rosalia NOAA weather station (Station ID: {{ NOAA_Station_ID_Rosalia }}) for the period 2022 to 2024 was correlated with influent flow data recorded at the Tekoa’s WWTP as reported in DMRs. The Rosalia station is considered representative for regional precipitation patterns affecting the service area.

Wet Weather Flow (WWF) is qualitatively defined as the significantly increased and often rapidly fluctuating flow observed at the WWTP during and immediately following a rainfall event. This flow includes the normal ADWF components (sanitary flow and baseline groundwater infiltration) plus the additional, often substantial, volume contributed by RDII. The magnitude, timing (how quickly the flow responds to rain), and duration of the WWF response depend on numerous factors. These include rainfall intensity, total rainfall volume, and duration; antecedent moisture conditions (how saturated the ground was before the storm); the physical condition and age of the sewer system (more defects lead to more I&I); soil types and geology; groundwater levels; the topography of the service area; and the extent of directly connected impervious surfaces.

The analysis of WWF for {{ Name_of_Town/Utility }} involved identifying peak flow events from the DMR data that corresponded with significant rainfall events recorded at the Rosalia station. For example, during the storm event of {{ Date_of_Peak_WWF_Storm }}, which recorded {{ Rainfall_Amount_Peak_WWF_Storm }} inches of rain, the peak WWF per capita was calculated to be {{ Peak_WWF_per_Capita_Value }} gpcd. The EPA guidance (for instance, from I/I analysis handbooks) often suggests that peak wet weather flows exceeding approximately 275 gpcd may be indicative of excessive I/I within a system. <<check>> This 275 gpcd is a general guideline and local conditions or specific permit requirements might dictate other thresholds for "excessive." <<check>> The calculated peak WWF of {{ Peak_WWF_per_Capita_Value }} gpcd for {{ Name_of_Town/Utility }} {{ Comparison_to_EPA_275_gpcd, for instance, "is below this EPA guideline, suggesting that while RDII is clearly present and contributes to peak flows, it might not be categorized as 'excessive' by this particular metric alone. However, any amount of RDII consumes capacity and should be managed," or "approaches this EPA guideline, indicating that RDII is a significant concern and likely warrants focused mitigation efforts to prevent future capacity issues or SSOs," or "exceeds this EPA guideline, strongly

nodes.text_clean: Mathematically, the total Wet Weather Flow (WWF) during a storm event can be expressed by considering RDII along with the base flow components: $$ \text{WWF} = Q_s + Q_{GWI} + Q_{RDII} $$ Where: * WWF = Total Wet Weather Flow (for instance, in gpd) during the event. * \( Q_s \) (gpd) = Base Sanitary Flow (gpd) – the component from user discharges. * \( Q_{GWI} \) (gpd) = Groundwater Infiltration (gpd). \( Q_{GWI} \) is the persistent infiltration component typically present even in dry weather, though it may increase during wet weather due to higher groundwater tables. * \( Q_{RDII} \) (gpd) = Rainfall-Derived Infiltration and Inflow is the additional flow directly attributable to the rainfall event. To perform the wet weather flow analysis for Tekoa’s collection system, daily precipitation data from the Rosalia NOAA weather station (Station ID: {{ NOAA_Station_ID_Rosalia }}) for the period 2022 to 2024 was correlated with influent flow data recorded at the Tekoa’s WWTP as reported in DMRs. The Rosalia station is considered representative for regional precipitation patterns affecting the service area. Wet Weather Flow (WWF) is qualitatively defined as the significantly increased and often rapidly fluctuating flow observed at the WWTP during and immediately following a rainfall event. This flow includes the normal ADWF components (sanitary flow and baseline groundwater infiltration) plus the additional, often substantial, volume contributed by RDII. The magnitude, timing (how quickly the flow responds to rain), and duration of the WWF response depend on numerous factors. These include rainfall intensity, total rainfall volume, and duration; antecedent moisture conditions (how saturated the ground was before the storm); the physical condition and age of the sewer system (more defects lead to more I&I); soil types and geology; groundwater levels; the topography of the service area; and the extent of directly connected impervious surfaces. nodes.text:
(AWWF) highest daily flow during and immediately after a significant storm event, including sanitary flow, infiltration, and inflow
  • conveyance and treatment systems.
  • Guides design of treatment units like aeration tanks, sedimentation basins, clarifiers, and pumping stations.
  • Used to calculate Peak Wet Weather Flow Factor (PWWF)
Peak Wet Weather Flow (PWWF) The highest flow rate observed during a wet weather event, typically over a 2-hour period
  • Flooding and overflow management during wet weather,
  • Sizing treatment plant to accommodate the additional flow caused by wet weather
  • Regulatory compliance to minimize risk of contamination and overflow
Maximum Week Flow The highest 7-day average flow that occurs within a one-year period
  • Primarily used for sizing treatment plant units such as: Bioreactors, Clarifiers, Aeration basins, Settling tanks
  • Energy Consumption and Operational Planning
  • Disinfection System Design
  • Sludge Management
Maximum Month Flow (MMF) The Maximum Month Flow is the highest 30-day average flow that occurs within a one-year period.
  • Same as Maximum Week Flow
Peak Hour Design Flow (PHDF) The highest flow anticipated to occur during a one-hour period, expressed as a daily or hourly average.
  • Primarily used for sizing pumps, interceptor sewers, flow meters, bar screens and grit channels.
  • To check the retention period for many critical treatment units.
  • Used to design the hydraulics of the influent and effluent structures of
Peak Instantaneous Design Flow (PIDF) The maximum anticipated instantaneous flow during peak conditions.
  • Used for sizing valves, pumps, and other infrastructure that need to withstand brief surges.
Minimum Flow The lowest flow observed in a system. Low flow may cause settling of solids in pipes and channels
  • Used to size flowmeters, chemical feeder and pumping equipment
*Tchobanoglous, G., Burton, F.L. and Stensel, H.D. (2003) Wastewater Engineering, Treatment and Reuse. 4th Edition, McGraw-Hill, Boston.

# 5.3 Wastewater Flow Summary *WAC 173-240-050(3)(g)* Discharge Monitoring Reports (DMRs) from 2022 through 2024 were analyzed to determine historical influent flows to WWTP for the design period. This data, which represents actual measured flows to and from the facility (taken daily), provides the basis for characterizing system hydraulics in this report. The flow data along with OFM population data, was used to generate **Table 5.3-1**, which summarizes total system flows, average daily flows, estimated total Equivalent Residential Units (ERUs), and the average daily flow per ERU. The table is followed by a

(MG/Year)		 Average Daily Flow (ADF)

(gpd/ERU)

2023 42.87 117,452 460 255

Guidance for ERU estimation and flow analysis was taken from {{ Cite_Guidance_Document, for instance, Washington State Department of Ecology's "Criteria for Sewage Works Design" (Orange Book) }}. The Orange Book provides standardized methodologies and typical values used in Washington State, ensuring consistency in engineering assessments. <> The average annual flow to the WWTP over this period was calculated to be {{ Calculated_Average_Annual_Flow }} gpd. This value is utilized for long-term operational planning, estimating annual O&M costs, and as a fundamental baseline for developing flow projections and assessing overall plant loading against its design capacity.

\n---\n # General Considerations The hydraulic capacity of the treatment works should be based on the maximum expected flow. The process design of treatment units should be based on either the average design flow or the peak design flow, whichever is controlling. The following items should be determined from the observed rate of flow during the significant period of discharge. Items to be considered in determining design flows are as follows: * Peak flow rates continuing over a length of time sufficient to adversely affect the detention time of treatment units or the flow characteristics in conduits. * Applicable data from similar municipalities. * Wet weather flows. * Recirculation and inplant recycle flows.

Parameter Definition Source Equation / Derivation
DWF “All flow in a sewer (includes sanitary flow and infiltration) except that caused directly by rainfall. Measured during a period of extended dry weather (7 to 14 days) and seasonally high groundwater.” EPA I/I Guide (2014) QDWF = QS + QGWI
Infiltration Infiltration – “Water other than sanitary wastewater that enters a sewer system from the ground through defective pipes, pipe joints, connections, or manholes. Infiltration does not include inflow.” EPA I/I Guide (2014)
Inflow Inflow - “Water other than sanitary wastewater that enters a sewer system from sources such as roof leaders, cellar/foundation drains, yard drains, area drains, drains from springs and swampy areas, manhole covers, cross connections between storm sewers and sanitary sewers, and catch basins. Inflow does not include infiltration.” EPA I/I Guide (2014)
Total I/I “Infiltration plus inflow - total extraneous wastewater entering the system.” 40 CFR § 35.2005(b)(20–21) QI/I = QDWF – BSF

### *Sanitary Flow Contribution*  
Sanitary flows are largely a function of population, population density, water consumption, and land uses. Hence, sanitary flow estimation usually involves a study of existing and projected land uses and demographic data, and water consumption from which to estimate per capita daily water consumption. The per capita wastewater flows are usually expressed as percentages (or “return ratio”) of per capita water consumptions. The “return ratios” for residential and non-residential uses may vary. These ratios can be determined from a careful analysis of a long-term (e.g., a full year) monitored sewer flow data and water consumption data taking into consideration of groundwater infiltration flows. In estimating probable future per capita wastewater contributions, changes in indoor and outdoor water use habits from the growing water conservation ethic and associated requirements should be considered. Special consideration needs to be given to industrial contributions as the rates vary with the type, size, and operation of the industry. Furthermore, peak discharges may be the result of flows contributed over a short operation time of the industry. Flows important to the design of sanitary sewers are daily minimum and maximum, daily average, and peak flow. Peak flows estimated for the end of the design period

---

usually determine the desired hydraulic capacities of sanitary sewers, pumps, and some treatment plant conduits (ASCE, 1982).

**Groundwater Infiltration (GWI)**  
GWI refers to that portion of wastewater embedded in the monitored dry‑weather flow (DWF) data at a sewage pump station or a treatment facility. The rate of GWI depends on the number and size of defects within a sewer and the hydraulic head available and hence is greater in wet spring high groundwater table season than in other seasons. As sewer pipes age and deteriorate, GWI is expected to increase in amount and scope. GWI problems can be particularly severe in areas where sewer systems are installed below the groundwater table. This unwanted flow results in the need for larger sewerage and increased O&M costs for pumping and treatment. Hence, in the design of sewers, it is customary to include a rate of about 1,000 gal/acre·d, some to require as high as 2,000 gal/acre·d (ASCE, 1982).

GWI rates can vary a great deal in a sewer system and local flow monitoring data are needed for determination of GWI rates that meaningfully reflect the gross site‑specific conditions. If permanent monitoring stations do not already exist, temporary monitoring stations are installed for flow data collection. The best time for flow monitoring for BWF and GWI determinations would be in early spring when groundwater table is high and outdoor water uses are low. During this time, wastewater from a residential area may be assumed to be the same as the billed water use and the GWI can be calculated as the difference between the measured DWF and the wastewater flow determined from the billed water use.

**Rainfall-Derived Infiltration/Inflow (RDII)**  
RDII is that portion of a sewer flow hydrograph above the normal dry‑weather base flow pattern. It is a sewer flow response to rainfall or snowmelt in a sewershed. The term of “I/I” probably first appeared in the 1960s. Prior to that time, the main focus was the determination of “infiltration” in DWF. “Inflow,” referred to as the “stormwater” contribution, was recognized but was only casually addressed (ASCE, 1962; HES, 1968). With the influx of federal money and enforcement of federal requirements in mid‑1970s, the amount of “I/I” data surged as did the interest of flow prediction of RDII from monitoring data particularly since late 1980s.

RDII has long been recognized as a major factor in the sizing of sewer pipes and treatment plants. It was found by tests that as much as 150 gal/min may leak through a manhole cover as stormwater inflow (Rawn, 1936). With manholes placed 300 to 500 ft apart, this would amount to 3.5 to 2.0 Mgal/mi∙d of sewer pipe (Babbitt, 1947). It was recognized in the early 20th century that improper connections of roof drains, street inlets, and foundation and cellar drains to sanitary sewers, in combination with poor quality of house lateral construction, depleted the reserved sewer capacities that were usually built‑in for the future growth of an area.

# C1-1 GENERAL REQUIREMENTS

## C1-1.1 APPROVALS
Designs of new sewer systems or extensions of existing systems must provide for:
* Adequately processing the added hydraulic and organic load; or
* Adequately providing treatment facilities on a time schedule acceptable to Ecology.
See Chapter G1 for additional information on approvals.

## C1-1.2 OWNERSHIP (CERTIFICATION BY THE ENGINEER OR THE OWNER)
Sewer systems shall be owned and operated by a public entity demonstrating the capability to operate and maintain the sewer system.

## C1-1.3 DESIGN
Sewer systems shall be designed and constructed to achieve total containment of sanitary wastes and maximum exclusion of infiltration and inflow (I/I). No new combined sewers will be approved.

See Chapter G1 for additional information on approvals. ## C1-1.2 OWNERSHIP (CERTIFICATION BY THE ENGINEER OR THE OWNER) Sewer systems shall be owned and operated by a public entity demonstrating the capability to operate and maintain the sewer system. ## C1-1.3 DESIGN Sewer systems shall be designed and constructed to achieve total containment of sanitary wastes and maximum exclusion of infiltration and inflow (I/I). No new combined sewers will be approved.

The process design of treatment units should be based on either the average design flow or the peak design flow, whichever is controlling. The following items should be determined from the observed rate of flow during the significant period of discharge. Items to be considered in determining design flows are as follows: * Peak flow rates continuing over a length of time sufficient to adversely affect the detention time of treatment units or the flow characteristics in conduits. * Applicable data from similar municipalities. * Wet weather flows. * Recirculation and inplant recycle flows. The design organic loading should be computed in the same manner used in determining design flow. ## G2-1.2.3 Existing Systems Treatment plants designed to serve existing sewerage systems should be designed on the basis of characteristics of sewage obtained from the operating records of the treatment works. The design engineer or owner shall provide a plan acceptable to Ecology for eliminating or handling excessive inflow/infiltration (I/I) so that there will be no discharge of inadequately treated wastewaters or impairment of the treatment process. ## G2-1.2.4 New Systems Sewage treatment plants to serve new sewerage systems should be designed on the basis of information in Table G2‑ 2. Numbers of persons per dwelling should be based on planning projections derived from an official source. Any deviations should be based on sound engineering judgment substantiated in the engineering report. Table G2- 2. Design Basis for New Sewage Works ## Table G2- 2. Design Basis for New Sewage Works
